Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2021

## DO ANDROIDS DREAM OF ELECTRIC FENCES? SAFETY-AWARE REINFORCEMENT LEARNING WITH LATENT SHIELDING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The growing trend of fledgling reinforcement learning systems making their way into real-world applications has been accompanied by growing concerns for their safety and robustness. In recent years, a variety of approaches have been put forward to address the challenges of safety-aware reinforcement learning; however, these methods often either require a handcrafted model of the environment to be provided beforehand, or that the environment is relatively simple and lowdimensional. We present a novel approach to safety-aware deep reinforcement learning in high-dimensional environments called _latent shielding_ . Latent shielding leverages internal representations of the environment learnt by model-based agents to “imagine” future trajectories and avoid those deemed unsafe. We experimentally demonstrate that this approach leads to improved adherence to formallydefined safety specifications.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

The steady trickle of reinforcement learning (RL) systems making their way out of the lab and into the real world has cast a spotlight on the safety and robustness of RL agents. The motivation behind this should be relatively easy to grasp: when training an agent in real-world settings, it is desirable that some states are never reached as they could, for instance, cause permanent damage to the hardware the agent is controlling. We can thus informally define the notion of _safety-aware RL_ in terms of the classical RL setup with the added requirement that the number of _unsafe_ states visited be minimised. Under this definition, however, it has been found that many state-of-the-art RL algorithms unnecessarily enter unsafe states despite safe alternatives being available and there being a positive correlation between avoiding such states and reward (Giacobbe et al., 2021).

The field of safety-aware RL encompasses a multitude of approaches ranging from constrained policy optimisation (Chow et al., 2017; Achiam et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2020) to safety critics (Srinivasan et al., 2020; Bharadhwaj et al., 2021; Thananjeyan et al., 2021) to meta-learning (Turchetta et al., 2020). In this work, we focus on a particular family of approaches known as _shielding_ (Alshiekh et al., 2018; Anderson et al., 2020; Giacobbe et al., 2021; ElSayed-Aly et al., 2021; Pranger et al., 2021). Central to shielding is the notion of a _shield_ , a filter that checks actions proposed by the agent’s existing policy with reference to a model of the environment’s dynamics and some formal safety specification. The shield overrides actions that may lead to an unsafe state using some other safe (but by no means optimal) policy. A key advantage of many shielding approaches is that the resulting _shielded_ policies are formally verifiable; however, a shortcoming is that they require a model of environmental dynamics - typically handcrafted - to be provided in advance. Providing such a model may prove difficult for complex real-world environments, with inaccuracies and human biases creeping into handcrafted models.

In this work, we propose a safe RL agent that makes uses of _latent shielding_ , an approach to shielding in environments where a formally-specified dynamics model is not available in advance. At an intuitive level, the agent uses a data-driven approach to learn its own latent world model (a component of which is a dynamics model) which is then leveraged by a shield. The shield then uses the agent’s model to “imagine” trajectories arising from different actions, forcing the agent to avoid

those it foresees leading to unsafe states. In addition, the agent can be trained within its own latent world model thus reducing the number of safety violations seen during training.

**Contributions** The main contribution of this work is a framework for shielding agents in complex, stochastic and high-dimensional environments without knowledge of environmental dynamics _a priori_ . We further introduce a new method to aid exploration when training shielded agents. Though our framework loses the formal safety guarantees associated with traditional symbolic shielding approaches, our experiments illustrate that latent shielding reduces unsafe behaviour during training and achieves testing performance comparable to previous symbolic approaches.

## 2 PRELIMINARIES

In this section, we cover some relevant background topics. We begin by introducing our problem setup for safety-aware RL and give an overview of the specification language used in this work. This is followed by an outline of the latent world model we make use of in this work as well as a discussion on shielding.

## 2.1 PROBLEM SETUP

We consider an agent interacting with an environment _E_ modelled as a _partially observable Markov decision process (POMDP)_ with states _s ∈SE_ , observations _ot ∈OE_ , agent-generated actions _at ∈AE_ and scalar rewards _rt ∈_ R over discrete time steps _t ∈_ [0 _,_ 1 _, ..., T −_ 1]. We assume the environment has been augmented with a labelling function _L[φ] E_[:] _[S][E][→{][safe, unsafe][}]_[that,] at each time step, informs us whether a _violation_ has occurred with respect to some formal safety specification _φ_ . For the avoidance of doubt, we define a violation to have occurred whenever _φ_ does not hold. This is a weaker assumption than previous works in shielding (which assume access to an abstraction of the environment) and can be thought as a secondary safety-focused reward function with a binary output. Intuitively, the goal of the agent is to learn a policy _π_ that maximises its expected cumulative reward while minimising the number of violations of _φ_ .

## 2.2 SYNTACTICALLY CO-SAFE LINEAR TEMPORAL LOGIC

In this work, we use _syntactically co-safe Linear Temporal Logic (scLTL)_ (Kupferman & Vardi, 2001) as our specification language. Valid scLTL formulae over some set of atomic propositions _AP_ can be constructed according to the following grammar:

_φ_ ::= _true | d | ¬d | φ ∨ φ | φ ∧ φ | ⃝φ | φ ∪ φ | ⋄φ_ (1)

where _d ∈ AP_ , _¬ (negation)_ , _∨ (disjunction)_ , _∧ (conjunction)_ are the familiar operators from propositional logic, and _⃝ (next)_ , _∪ (until)_ and _⋄ (eventually)_ are temporal operators. We can monitor a co-safe LTL specification using a technique known as _progression_ (Bacchus & Kabanza, 2000).

## 2.3 RECURRENT STATE-SPACE MODELS

We refer to the predictive model of an environment maintained by a model-based agent as its _world model_ . World models can be learnt from experience and be used both as a substitute for the environment during training (Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; Hafner et al., 2021) and for planning at run-time (Hafner et al., 2019b). Though many realisations of the notion of a world model exist, the world model used in this work is based on the _recurrent state-space model (RSSM)_ proposed by Hafner et al. (2019b).

An RSSM is composed of three key components: a latent dynamics model, a reward model, and an observation model. These components act on compact states formed from the concatenation of a deterministic latent state _ht_ and stochastic latent state _zt_ .

**Latent Dynamics Model** The latent dynamics model is made up of a number of smaller models. First, the _recurrent model ht_ = _f_ ( _ht−_ 1 _, zt−_ 1 _, at−_ 1) is used to compute the deterministic latent state based on the previous compact state and action. From _ht_ and the current observation _ot_ , a

distribution _q_ ( _zt|ht, ot_ ) over posterior stochastic latent states _zt_ is computed by the _representation model_ . At the same time, a distribution _p_ (ˆ _zt|ht_ ) over prior stochastic latent states _z_ ˆ _t_ is computed by the _transition model_ , based only on _ht_ . During training, the transition model attempts to minimise the _Kullback Leibler (KL)_ divergence between the prior and posterior stochastic latent state distributions. In doing this, the RSSM learns to predict future latent states (using the recurrent and transition models) without access to future observations.

**Observation Model** The _observation model_ computes the distribution _p_ (ˆ _ot|ht, zt_ ) over observations _o_ ˆ _t_ for a particular state. Though not strictly needed, the observation model can prove useful for visualising predicted future states and providing a richer training signal.
**Reward Model** The _reward model_ computes the distribution _p_ (ˆ _rt|ht, zt_ ) over rewards _r_ ˆ _t_ for a particular state.

In practice, the distributions _p_ and _q_ are implemented with neural networks _pθ_ and _qθ_ respectively, parameterised by some set of parameters _θ_ . These latent dynamics models define a fully-observable _Markov decision process (MDP)_ as the latent states in the agent’s own internal model can always be observed by the agent (Hafner et al., 2019a). We denote the state space of this MDP (comprised of compact latent states) as _SI_ .

## 2.4 SHIELDING

The _classical_ formulation of shielding in RL is given by Alshiekh et al. (2018). It assumes access to two ingredients: an LTL safety specification and _abstraction_ (a MDP model of the environment that captures the aspects of the environment relevant for planning ahead with respect to the safety specification). These ingredients are used to construct a formally verifiable reactive system that monitors the agent’s actions, overriding those which lead to violation states.

Proposed by Giacobbe et al. (2021), _bounded prescience shielding (BPS)_ avoids the need for handcrafted abstractions by exploiting the fact that some agents are trained in computer simulations. The shield operates by leveraging access to the program underlying the simulation to look ahead into future states within some finite horizon. Using BPS over classical shielding does, however, come with a few disadvantages. Firstly, it requires access to the simulation at run-time which may prove difficult to provide (especially in cases where running the simulation is computationally expensive). Moreover, an agent using BPS, even when starting from a safe state, can find itself entering unsafe states in cases where the number of steps between a violation being caused by an action and the violation state itself exceeds the shield’s look-ahead horizon. This is not the case for classical shielding which resembles BPS with an infinite horizon.

## 2.5 BOUNDED SAFETY

The notion of safety used by BPS is defined over MDPs. For an arbitrary MDP with states _S_ and actions _A_ , a _bounded trajectory ρ_ of length _H_ is a sequence of states and actions _s_ 0 _−→a_ 0 _s_ 1 _−→a_ 1 _an−_ 1 _. . . −−−→ sn_ comprised of no more than _H_ states and with the final state _sn_ either being a terminal state or _n_ = _H −_ 1. We further denote the set of all finite trajectories starting from some arbitrary state _s ∈S_ by _ϱ_ ( _s_ ) and the set of all bounded trajectories of length _H_ that start from _s_ by _ϱH_ ( _s_ ).

We say a bounded trajectory _ρ_ of length _H_ satisfies _H-bounded safety_ with respect to safety specification _φ_ , written _SH_ ( _ρ, φ_ ), if and only if for all _si ∈ ρ_ , _L[φ] E_[(] _[s][i]_[) =] _[ safe]_[.][Moreover, we can extend] the notion of _H_ -bounded safety over the set of policies: a policy _π_ is _H-bounded safe_ with respect to _φ_ , denoted as _SH_ ( _π, φ_ ), if and only if for all s _∈S_ ,

- either there exists some _ρ ∈ ϱH_ ( _s_ ) such that _SH_ ( _ρ, φ_ ) and _π_ ( _s_ 0) = _a_ 0;

- or for all _ρ ∈ ϱH_ ( _s_ ), _¬SH_ ( _ρ, φ_ ) _._

In other words, the policy will choose a safe trajectory as long as one exists. Finally, we formally define a violation of _φ_ to be _inevitable_ in state _s_ 0 _∈S_ if and only if for all _ρ ∈ ϱ_ ( _s_ 0), _¬SH_ ( _ρ, φ_ ).

## 3 SHIELDED DREAMS

We introduce the notion of _latent shielding_ , a novel class of shielding approaches that replace abstractions used by shields with learned latent dynamics models thus allowing the enforcement of formal safety specifications while avoiding the need for an explicitly-defined abstraction of the environment. We further introduce the first such approach, _approximate bounded prescience shielding_ , a framework for latent shielding that leverages latent world models learnt by model-based _deep RL (DRL)_ agents. In this work, our model-based agent of choice is Dreamer (Hafner et al., 2021)[1] , which we modify to incorporate shielding into its data collection, training and deployment phases.

## 3.1 SAFETY RSSM

We augment the standard RSSM with a labelling function _L[φ] I_[:] _[ S][I][→{][safe, unsafe][}]_[ which maps] latent states _st ∈SI_ to whether they correspond to states in violation of _φ_ . As with the other models, _L[φ] I_[is implemented with a neural network with a categorical output] _[ l][θ]_[also parameterised by] _[ θ]_[.][This] yields an enhanced RSSM (illustrated in Figure 1) which we will refer to as a _safety RSSM (SRSSM)_ .

The inclusion of the weighting hyperparameter _α_ is motivated by the observation that the number of observations of safe and unsafe states will generally be quite imbalanced. Thus, to prevent predictions by _lθ_ from collapsing to a single class, we assign a greater weight to the less-represented class.

## 3.2 APPROXIMATE BOUNDED PRESCIENCE SHIELDING

We now integrate the SRSSM as part of a latent shielding approach which we shall refer to as _approximate bounded prescience shielding (ABPS)_ . Though ABPS is inspired by BPS, it differs in

two key aspects: (1) we approximate the labelling function _L[φ] E_[and environmental dynamics using an] SRSSM; and (2) we sample a fixed number of potential future trajectories as opposed to exhaustively exploring all possibilities.

Thus, our approach can be thought of as an approximation of some “ideal” bounded prescience shield that uses the true environmental dynamics and labelling function. The advantage of the first difference should be obvious: it enables the shield to learn its own abstraction, removing the need for hand-crafting or access to a digital environment’s underlying program. Why the second difference is advantageous may be slightly less obvious - it’s a heuristic that allows us to increase the horizon _H_ . By directing the sampling of trajectories in accordance with states and actions the policy is biased towards (as opposed to uniformly), it may be possible to achieve comparable performance to a standard BPS in foreseeing unsafe states. The intuition behind this is that by not sampling trajectories the agent is unlikely to take, more of our computational budget can be dedicated into ensuring that the agent’s most likely trajectories are safe. In other words, we will not spend time planning to correct actions that the agent is unlikely to take.

More formally, our shielded policy _π[∗]_ can be written:

where _π_ ( _st_ ) is the agent’s policy without shielding; _st ∈SI_ is some compact latent state; and _πalt_ is an alternative policy that ensures that, if a violation isn’t already inevitable, the agent avoids a predicted unsafe state. In our implementation, _πalt_ simply considers all the other actions until a safe trajectory is found. In the event that no safe action is found, the agent takes a random action.

Since the SRSSM models stochasticity, we can sample multiple futures arising from an action being taken and derive probabilistic estimates of whether an action will lead to a violation. In this way, we can estimate the safety of an action taken in a given state by checking whether the probability of a violation occurring (inferred by sampling) exceeds a fixed threshold _ϵ_ (see Algorithm 1). Moreover, the sampling process can, in practice, be augmented to sample a wider range of trajectories (less likely to be taken by the agent) by adding a small amount of noise to actions suggested by the policy during sampling.

3.3 TRAINING REGIME

We extend the training regime proposed in Hafner et al. (2019a) to include the training and application of ABPS. Though the full training procedure is detailed in Algorithm 2, we provide an overview below.

## 3.3.1 OVERVIEW

The training procedure can be split into three phases: data collection, latent world model training and agent training (lines 10-22, 4-6 and 7-8 in Algorithm 2 respectively). These phases are cycled through until convergence.

**Data Collection** In this phase, the agent interacts with the real environment to collect a dataset _D ⊆S × A × O ×_ R _× {_ 0 _,_ 1 _}_ of states, actions, observations, rewards and violations with which a latent world model can be learned. At the very start of training, we collect trajectories from _S_ seed episodes using a random policy; at all other times, we use the agent’s shielded policy. An illustration of the components of the agent active during this phase can be found in Figure 4 in Appendix A.

**Latent World Model Training** The goal of this phase is to improve the model of the world so that the policy has an accurate imagined environment to train in. To this end, we train the latent world model with respect to the objective in Equation 2 on _B_ data sequences of length _L_ sampled from _D_ . An illustration of the components of the agent active during this phase can be found in Figure 5 in Appendix A.

**Agent Training** Agent training is composed of two steps. First, starting from states from the same _B_ data sequences from the latent world model training phase, the agent imagines _I_ trajectories of length _H_ with actions chosen from its current policy. Next, the unshielded policy _π_ is updated as in Hafner et al. (2019a; 2021).

## 3.3.2 KEY CHANGES AND CONTRIBUTIONS

We describe and discuss the key changes we have made that differentiate our agent from previous approaches.
**Experience Dataset.** Elements in the experience dataset _D_ now contain a binary variable representing whether a violation has occurred _λt_ .

**Latent Shielding.** Before being sent to the environment _E_ , actions _at_ generated by the unshielded policy _π_ are routed through our newly-proposed latent shield (described in Algorithm 1). The shield returns a new approximately _H_ -bounded safe action _a[′] t_[.]

**Intrinsic Punishment.** Whenever a violation occurs (whether detected by the latent shield or by the environment), we override the environment’s reward function by instead assigning a reward _p <_ 0. This discourages the agent’s unshielded policy _π_ from taking actions that lead to unsafe states through the standard RL setup. Over time, this means that the shield will have to interfere less as _π_ becomes biased away from unsafe states. This is a standard practice in the shielding literature (Alshiekh et al., 2018), however new to the Dreamer family of agents (Hafner et al., 2019a;b; 2021).

**Shield Introduction Schedule** We introduce the novel notion of a _shield introduction schedule_ Π which can enable and disable shielding during training. The rationale behind this is that a shield backed by an inaccurate world model will incorrectly label some safe states as unsafe, and vice versa. In some cases, this may prove detrimental to the training process: suppose _lθ_ happens to be initialised in such a way that all states are labelled as unsafe. As a result, the shield can prevent the agent from exploring the environment and improving its internal model of the environment. This, in turn, can prevent the labelling function from learning to correctly differentiate safe states from unsafe states.

To give the agent time to learn a good world model before restricting exploration through a learned shield, we augment the training procedure with Π which aims to gradually introduce shielding. To our knowledge, this is the first time such a system has been proposed. Though there exist a wide range of possible implementations of Π, in this work we use a simple schedule that seems to work well empirically: start training with shielding disabled and enable shielding once the world model loss begins to plateau (see Appendix H for more details). A detailed comparison of different shield introduction schedules, however, is beyond the scope of this study and left for further work.

## 4 EXPERIMENTS

In this section, we compare our ABPS agent against Dreamer without shielding (Hafner et al., 2019a; 2021), Dreamer with BPS (Giacobbe et al., 2021), and CPO (Achiam et al., 2017). We also empirically investigate some aspects of the internal workings of our agent. A brief overview of the environments used can be found below with more detailed a discussion in Appendix C.

**Visual Grid World** The _Visual Grid World (VGW)_ environment is a simple deterministic navigation benchmark with high-dimensional (64 _×_ 64 _×_ 3) visual observations (see Figure 2) and discrete actions. The agent’s (in green) task is to navigate to randomly-placed targets (in black) while avoiding any unsafe locations (in red). We experiment with both _fixed_ and _procedurally generated_ grids. **Cliff Driver** The _Cliff Driver (CD)_ environment is a symbolic benchmark with stochastic dynamics and continuous actions. The agent controls the forward acceleration of a car and is tasked with driving to the edge of a cliff as quickly as possible without overshooting and falling into the sea. Stochasticity comes from the fact that at each time step there is a probability _pstick_ that car’s controls become “stuck” (Machado et al., 2018).

As can be seen in Table 4, our agent collected more reward at test-time than both the unshielded and BPS agents in the VGW environment (reward curves during training can be seen in Figure 3 with further training curves showcased in Appendix E). Moreover, our agent saw a seven-fold reduction in test-time violations compared to the unshielded agent across both the fixed and procedurally generated environments, averaging at less than one violation per episode for the fixed VGW. This reduction in training violations is even more dramatic when compared to the model-free CPO agent. Our agent outperformed the other agents at test-time in the most stochastic CD environment ( _pstick_ = 0 _._ 5). This is possibly due to the SRSSM used by our latent shield being better able to capture non-determinism than BPS. Nevertheless, the result is still rather impressive given that the agent using BPS sampled 1024 trajectories at every time step whereas our agent only sampled 20.

## 4.2 OTHER EXPERIMENTS

**Qualitative Evaluation of Learned Latent Dynamics** We evaluate the quality of each agent’s latent dynamics model by observing its trajectory predictions given a particular starting state and action sequence. Each agent is given the same starting observation and predefined sequence of 10 actions. The agents take these actions in their respective latent world models with the states traversed being decoded for inspection. Results can be seen in Appendix G and indicate that, qualitatively, our agent’s decoder performed the best, accurately predicting 10 frames into the future. One possible reason for this observation is that the inclusion of the violation detection objective in the SRSSM encourages the model to focus on accurately capturing violation states. Another potential factor is that the BPS agent never actually enters unsafe states and thus finds it difficult to represent them.

## 5 RELATED WORK AND DISCUSSION

**Latent World Models** Learning latent world models from visual observations has seen growing interest from the RL community (Wahlstr¨om et al., 2015; Watter et al., 2015; Racani`ere et al., 2017; Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; Hafner et al., 2019a;b; Schrittwieser et al., 2020; Hafner et al., 2021). One key trend in the literature is that of training agents in their own learned world models (Ha & Schmidhuber, 2018; Hafner et al., 2019a; 2021). In this work, we extend the world model formulation used in Hafner et al. (2019a;b; 2021) to encode a notion of safety into state representations.

**Safety By Filtering Actions** Overriding unsafe actions with safe ones has been a popular approach to safety-aware RL. Introduced into the RL scene by Alshiekh et al. (2018), shielding has received much research interest and has seen applications in real-world settings (Nikou et al., 2021). Various works have attempted to address some of its shortcomings of the original formulation. These include allowing the shield to be updated to aid exploration or correct model imperfections (Anderson et al., 2020; Pranger et al., 2021); improving performance in non-deterministic environments (Jansen et al., 2020; Li & Bastani, 2020); and extending shielding to multi-agent RL (ElSayed-Aly et al., 2021). To our knowledge, few works (Srinivasan et al., 2020; Thananjeyan et al., 2021; Bharadhwaj et al., 2021; Giacobbe et al., 2021) focus on removing the need for handcrafting an abstraction (among the most time-consuming and error-prone aspects of shielding), and only one of them (Giacobbe et al., 2021) achieves this without getting rid of the abstraction altogether, albeit by providing the agent with access to the program that controls the environment.

In contrast, latent shielding tackles the problem head-on by directly learning an abstraction for use by the shield. In this work, the abstraction we use is an SRSSM, which captures stochasticity by design, a useful property for non-deterministic environments. Though our latent shield satisfies an approximation of _H_ -bounded safety (see Section 2.4) with respect to the learned abstraction, its safety with respect to the true environmental dynamics is not guaranteed and instead relies on the fidelity with which the true dynamics are captured. Furthermore, unlike for its formally-verified predecessors, it is a necessary sacrifice that the agent visits unsafe states during training in order to learn a notion of safety (unless, of course, pre-training is possible). Nevertheless, a learned abstraction may be advantageous in settings where handcrafting an abstraction is not feasible and privileged access to some simulation (as in Giacobbe et al. (2021)) cannot be assumed. Moreover, latent shielding may provide a greater degree of explainability over model-free methods which get rid of the abstraction altogether (Srinivasan et al., 2020; Thananjeyan et al., 2021; Bharadhwaj et al., 2021) - if the shield overrides an action, one can reconstruct the imagined unsafe trajectories that led to the interference.

## 6 CONCLUSIONS

In this paper, we have presented _latent shielding_ , a new framework for shielding DRL agents without the need for a handcrafted abstraction of the environment. Using this framework, we have designed a novel shield and demonstrated that this method not only leads to improved adherence to safety specifications on two benchmark environments with respect to an unshielded agent, but also works out-of-the-box on both continuous and discrete environments (unlike its predecessor, BPS). Furthermore, we have demonstrated for the first time that shielding at inappropriate times may adversely impact the performance of model-based DRL agents and showed how this phenomenon can be counteracted using our novel notion of _shield introduction schedules_ .

Our work opens several exciting avenues for future work. For instance, this work uses a very simple shield introduction schedule; future work may provide a richer investigation into the properties of different schedules. Moreover, though demonstrating promising empirical results, our realisation of latent shielding loses the formally verifiable safety guarantees enjoyed by many symbolic shielding approaches - whether it is possible to construct a verifiable implementation of latent shielding, or compensate for the loss of formal guarantees, are open problems.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper is clearly motivated and addresses a well-defined gap in the literature: the trade-off between the formal verifiability of traditional symbolic shielding and the flexibility required for high-dimensional, unknown environments. The writing is technically precise and logically structured, particularly in its transition from the limitations of existing shielding methods to the proposed latent approach. However, the most significant risk—and one the authors honestly acknowledge—is the loss of formal safety guarantees. As a reviewer, the primary concern is the potential for "latent drift"; because the shield relies on a learned model that may be inaccurate, the agent’s safety performance is tethered to the model's predictive accuracy. To ensure scientific rigor, I would look for the remainder of the paper to provide a robust empirical characterization of this failure mode and an analysis of how the shield behaves when the latent world model is poorly calibrated or experiences aleatoric uncertainty, as this is the point where the proposed method deviates most from traditional, reliable shielding.

comments.quote: a bounded trajectory ρ of length H is a sequence of states and actions s_0 −→^{a_0} s_1 −→^{a_1} ... a_{n−1} −−−→ s_n comprised of no more than H states and with the final state s_n either being a terminal state or n = H − 1.
comments.explanation: The definition says 'no more than H states' and then gives the condition n = H − 1 (meaning H states indexed 0..H-1). However, it also says the final state is either terminal or n = H-1. If the trajectory terminates early (e.g., hits a terminal state before H steps), the trajectory length is less than H, which is consistent with 'no more than H states'. But the indexing condition 'n = H − 1' appears to contradict the possibility of early termination, because if n < H-1 the formula n = H-1 would be false. The intended meaning is likely that n ≤ H-1, but the text literally says n = H-1. A careful reader could be confused about whether early-terminated trajectories of length less than H are permitted.

comments.title: Potential mis-indexing in Algorithm 1 step 4
comments.quote: if ∃i ∈ [1, 2, ..., H] such that l_θ( h_i, z_i ) = unsafe then Λ[ n ] := 1.
comments.explanation: The imagined trajectory in step 3 has indices from 1 to H (starting after the initial state). The violation check iterates i from 1 to H. However, the imagined trajectory includes H steps of prediction (from initial state to state H), so there are H predicted states. This seems correct. But the pseudocode uses h_i and z_i without defining a notation for the deterministic and stochastic components at step i. In the text, the compact latent state is (h, z). So (h_i, z_i) is plausible. The minor issue is that the loop should also check the initial state (index 0) if the current state might already be unsafe; but the shield is presumably only called when starting from a safe state. This is a minor oversight but likely not a problem in practice.

comments.title: Unclear how alternative policy π_alt operates
comments.quote: π_alt simply considers all the other actions until a safe trajectory is found.
comments.explanation: The description of π_alt is vague. Does it try actions in a fixed order? Randomly? Does it re-sample trajectories for each candidate action using the same SRSSM? How does it define 'safe trajectory' - is it the same probabilistic threshold used in the main shield? The phrase 'until a safe trajectory is found' suggests iterative search, but no details are given about termination, computational budget, or fallback behavior beyond the last resort random action. This underspecification makes the method difficult to reproduce faithfully.

comments.title: Threshold comparison in Algorithm 1 line 7 uses ambiguous notation
comments.quote: if N[1]  λ∈ Λ [λ > ϵ] [ then]
comments.explanation: The notation in line 7 is garbled by OCR, but the intended meaning is likely 'if (1/N) Σ λ > ε' i.e., the fraction of unsafe trajectories exceeds a threshold. However, the text earlier says 'the probability of a violation occurring (inferred by sampling) exceeds a fixed threshold ε'. The notation in the algorithm should be something like 'if (1/N) Σ_{λ∈Λ} λ > ε'. The OCR artifact makes it impossible to read the exact intended expression. Given the context, the meaning is recoverable, but the garbled symbol could confuse a reader if the PDF is the primary source.

comments.title: Inconsistent definition of inevitable violation uses wrong set of trajectories
comments.quote: Finally, we formally define a violation of φ to be \textit{inevitable} in state s_0 \in S if and only if for all \rho \in \varrho(s_0), \neg S_H(\rho, \phi).
comments.explanation: The definition of inevitable violation uses the set ρ(s₀) (all finite trajectories), but the predicate S_H is defined only for bounded trajectories of length H (set ρ_H(s₀)). The authors likely intended ρ_H(s₀). Using the full set of unbounded trajectories is inconsistent because S_H is only defined for bounded trajectories of length H, and a trajectory longer than H would not be in the domain of S_H. This could mislead a reader trying to understand the formal safety guarantees. The context does not resolve this, as the definition is standalone; earlier in the same paragraph the policy definition correctly uses ρ_H, making this a clear oversight.

comments.title: Dataset notation uses environment state space S instead of latent state space S_I
comments.quote: where D_{\lambda} \subseteq S \times \{0,1\} is a dataset of state-label pairs
comments.explanation: The labelling function Lφ_I maps latent states S_I to safety labels, but the dataset D_λ is defined as a subset of S × {0,1}, where S is the environment state space from the POMDP definition. This is inconsistent because the neural network l_θ operates on latent states (compact states of the RSSM), not environment states. The authors likely intended S_I. This is a minor notation oversight.

comments.title: Alternative policy π_alt behavior is underspecified for reproducibility
comments.quote: In our implementation, \pi_{alt} simply considers all the other actions until a safe trajectory is found.
comments.explanation: The description of π_alt is vague. It says 'considers all the other actions until a safe trajectory is found.' This could mean that for each alternative action, the shield samples one or many trajectories; how does it determine that a safe trajectory is found? Algorithm 1 calls π_alt with (h_i, z_i) and expects it to return an action and interfered flag, but the internal logic is not specified (e.g., does it exhaustively check each action? How many samples per action? How does it handle stochastic dynamics?). While the high-level idea is clear, a reader attempting to reproduce the method would need more detail. This underspecification could lead to different implementations and affect results.

comments.title: Missing mathematical definition of shielded policy
comments.quote: More formally, our shielded policy \( \pi[^{*}] \) can be written:
comments.explanation: The text introduces a formal definition of the shielded policy and states 'More formally, our shielded policy π* can be written:', but the actual formula or definition is not provided in the passage. This appears to be a formatting issue where the equation was omitted (likely due to the PDF extraction). Without this definition, the precise relationship between the unshielded policy, the alternative policy, and the threshold is left undefined, making it difficult for a reader to understand the exact algorithmic decision rule.

comments.title: Ambiguity in sampling direction for safe trajectory search
comments.quote: In our implementation, \( \pi_{\text{alt}} \) simply considers all the other actions until a safe trajectory is found.
comments.explanation: The description of π_alt is ambiguous. 'Considers all the other actions until a safe trajectory is found' is unclear about the search order: (1) Does it evaluate each action independently, perhaps by sampling N trajectories per action? (2) Does it search actions in some fixed order? (3) What horizon is used for this search—the same H, or a shorter horizon? The algorithm later uses π_alt with arguments (h_i, z_i) but the text never specifies how many actions are evaluated or what happens if multiple actions appear safe. This underspecification could lead to different implementations producing different safety behavior.

comments.title: Possible subscript mismatch in π_alt call in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: **8** \( a, interfered := \pi_{\text{alt}} ( h_i, z_i ), true \)
comments.explanation: In Algorithm 1, line 8 calls π_alt with arguments (h_i, z_i). However, the variables h_i and z_i are not defined in the algorithm's scope. The preceding loop uses ĥ_i and ẑ_i (with hats) for imagined states. The subscript 'i' is also undefined—it likely refers to some index within the imagined trajectory, but it is not specified which one. The most plausible intended meaning is that the alternative policy is applied from the current latent state (h, z) (the input state), but the text uses (h_i, z_i) without a clear referent. This may be an OCR artifact or a residual from a previous draft.

comments.title: Inconsistent horizon notation between text and algorithm
comments.quote: Algorithm 1: ... horizon \( H \), number of trajectories to sample \( N \) ... **3** Imagine trajectory \( \rho = (h, z) \xrightarrow{a} (\hat{h}_1, \hat{z}_1) \xrightarrow{a_1} ... \xrightarrow{a_{H-1}} (\hat{h}_H, \hat{z}_H) \) where \( a_i = \pi(\hat{h}_i, z_i) \).
comments.explanation: The trajectory in Algorithm 1 has length H (with states indexed 1 to H), but the action sequence has H-1 actions (a through a_{H-1}). This is consistent with standard MDP trajectories of H steps (H states, H-1 actions). However, the earlier definition of bounded trajectories (Section 2.5) says a bounded trajectory of length H has 'no more than H states' and 'the final state either being a terminal state or n = H-1'. There is a slight notational tension: in the definition, length H refers to the number of states, while in Algorithm 1, the subscript on the last predicted state is H, implying H states. This is consistent, but the text earlier says 'a bounded trajectory ρ of length H is a sequence of states and actions s0 →a0 s1 →a1 ... a_{n-1}→ sn comprised of no more than H states'. If H is the maximum number of states, then the indices in Algorithm 1 (H states, H-1 actions) match. No actual error, just potential confusion for readers mapping between the general definition and the algorithm's usage.

comments.quote: **7 if** \( N[1] \; \lambda \in \Lambda [\lambda > \epsilon] \) **then**

comments.quote: **4 if** \( \exists i \in [1, 2, ..., H] \) such that \( l_\theta (h_i, z_i) = unsafe \) **then** \( \Lambda[n] := 1 \).

The variables h_i and z_i without hats are not defined in the loop. comments.title: OCR corruption in algorithmic step numbering

comments.explanation: Line 5 of Algorithm 2 appears corrupted by OCR. The intended text should be something like 'Draw B data sequences (ot, at, rt, λt) from D' or 'Compute model states ht, zt'. The current mix of symbols ('54', 'model states B data sequences h {t', 'rz ˆ tt, λ . t') is unreadable and does not match the surrounding structure. While the algorithm's intent is clear from context (the authors state that lines 4-6 are 'Latent World Model Training'), the corruption obscures a critical step needed for reproducibility.

comments.title: Ambiguity in punishment condition for interfered shield actions
comments.quote: 20 if λt = 1 or interfered then rt := rpunish .
comments.explanation: Line 20 of Algorithm 2 assigns punishment reward rpunish whenever the shield interfered, even if no actual violation occurred in the environment. Initially, this seems to conflate 'shield intervention' with 'true safety violation'. However, the context in Section 3.3.2 clarifies that 'Whenever a violation occurs (whether detected by the latent shield or by the environment), we override the environment's reward', which explicitly includes shield-detected violations. The issue is that the algorithm also punishes when the shield interferes but the environment would have been safe (false positive). While the authors may intend this as a conservative penalty, the text only mentions punishing 'whenever a violation occurs', which is subtly different from 'whenever the shield believes a violation might occur'. This ambiguity could mislead readers about the exact reward shaping scheme.

comments.title: Missing definition of violation detection threshold epsilon in Algorithm 1
comments.quote: 8 a, interfered := πalt (hi, zi) , true .
comments.explanation: Algorithm 1 uses a threshold epsilon in its decision rule (line 7: 'if (1/N) * sum(Λ) > epsilon'), but the detailed pseudocode for πalt and how it selects a safe action when all actions appear unsafe is underspecified. The text says 'πalt simply considers all the other actions until a safe trajectory is found. In the event that no safe action is found, the agent takes a random action.' This is vague: does πalt re-run the same probabilistic check for each alternative action? How many trajectories are sampled per alternative action? Without specifying these parameters, the algorithm is not fully reproducible.

comments.title: Potential inconsistency: shield horizon H used for both training imagination and shielding
comments.quote: In this work, we use a simple schedule that seems to work well empirically: start training with shielding disabled and enable shielding once the world model loss begins to plateau (see Appendix H for more details).
comments.explanation: The notation is overloaded: the same symbol H is used for the shield imagination horizon in Algorithm 1 and the imagination horizon I for training in Algorithm 2 (line 7). The text defines 'shield imagination horizon H' and 'number of steps to imagine ahead for training I' in the Algorithm 2 input list, but then in Section 3.3.2 the 'Shield Introduction Schedule' discusses plateau detection without clarifying which loss (world model loss or total loss) is monitored. Appendix H is referenced but not provided in the passage, so the reader cannot verify the claim. This underspecification makes the schedule non-reproducible.

comments.title: Overclaim about test-time violations in Visual Grid World
comments.quote: our agent saw a seven-fold reduction in test-time violations compared to the unshielded agent across both the fixed and procedurally generated environments, averaging at less than one violation per episode for the fixed VGW.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'seven-fold reduction' is not directly supported by numbers in Table 4. For fixed VGW, unshielded testing violations are 2.25 (1.60) and latent is 0.30 (0.76), which is actually a 7.5-fold reduction (2.25/0.30 = 7.5), so the arithmetic works. However, the standard deviations are large relative to the means (latent: 0.30 ± 0.76, unshielded: 2.25 ± 1.60), so the difference may not be statistically significant with only 5 runs. The text presents this as a definitive improvement without reporting confidence intervals or statistical tests. This is a mild overclaim given high variance.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper addresses a critical challenge in the field of safety-aware reinforcement learning by attempting to bridge the gap between theoretical guarantees and the practical need for high-dimensional, complex environments where handcrafted dynamic models are infeasible. The core contribution, "latent shielding," is conceptually sound as it leverages learned world models (specifically RSSMs) to simulate safety constraints internally, thereby removing the reliance on external symbolic models. The writing is clear and logically structured, effectively motivating the work by contrasting the limitations of traditional shielding with the proposed data-driven approach. However, the most significant issue to address concerns the theoretical trade-off introduced in the text: the paper admits that this framework loses the "formal safety guarantees" associated with traditional symbolic shielding due to the approximation errors inherent in learned dynamics. It is critical for the authors to rigorously justify how a learned model, subject to distributional shift and hallucination risks, can reliably act as a "shield" without introducing new safety vulnerabilities. Additionally, the reviewer needs to scrutinize the practical feasibility of the approach, as it relies on an oracle labeling function $L[\phi]$ to determine violations, a requirement that remains a substantial bottleneck in unstructured real-world settings despite being an improvement over requiring handcrafted abstractions.

comments.title: Underspecified initial data collection in training loop
comments.quote: Algorithm 2 description: Input... Initialise dataset D with S seed episodes... while not converged... Draw B... Imagine... Update... Select action... Step... Add...
comments.explanation: The text in section 3.3.1 explicitly states: 'At the very start of training, we collect trajectories from S seed episodes using a random policy; at all other times, we use the agent's shielded policy.' This implies a distinct initial data collection phase that is required to populate the dataset before training can begin on model updates (representation learning). However, the description of Algorithm 2 (lines 1-23) initializes D and immediately enters the main loop without showing this initial 'seed episode' collection step or explaining how the initial training of the latent model occurs using this seed data before the main agent policy training begins. While the initialization comment implies the episodes are already in D, the workflow for generating and using this initial data is not clear.

comments.title: Logical Inconsistency in H-bounded Safety Definition
comments.quote: Finally, we formally define a violation of φ to be inevitable in state s_0 ∈ S if and only if for all ρ ∈ ϱ(s_0), ¬SH(ρ, φ). [2.5] We say a bounded trajectory ρ of length H satisfies H-bounded safety with respect to safety specification φ, written SH(ρ, φ), if and only if for all si ∈ ρ, L[φ]E[(si) = safe. Moreover, we can extend the notion of H-bounded safety over the set of policies: a policy π is H-bounded safe with respect to φ, denoted as SH(π, φ), if and only if for all s ∈ S, either there exists some ρ ∈ ϱH(s) such that SH(ρ, φ) and π(s_0) = a_0; or for all ρ ∈ ϱH(s), ¬SH(ρ, φ).
comments.explanation: The definition of a safe policy contains a logical error. The 'or' clause allows a policy to be considered 'H-bounded safe' if all its possible trajectories lead to violations (i.e., the violation is inevitable). This contradicts the paper's motivation of 'safety-aware RL' to 'minimise the number of unsafe states' and the purpose of the shield, which is designed to prevent actions leading to unsafe states. A standard safety definition would require the policy to choose only safe trajectories, i.e., SH(π, φ) = (∃ safe trajectory ∈ ϱH(s) ∧ π(s_0) = action) ... . The definition provided effectively treats 'inevitable crash' as 'safe' if the crash was unavoidable.

comments.title: Inconsistent State Notation in Algorithm 1 Rollout
comments.quote: Imagine trajectory ρ = (h, z) −→ (ĥ_1 , ẑ_1) −→ ... −−−→ (ĥ_H , ẑ_H) where a_i = π(ĥ_i, z_i).
comments.explanation: The trajectory definition uses the predicted states `ĥ_i, ẑ_i` to define the flow (arrows). However, the equation defining the action selection for this trajectory uses `π(ĥ_i, z_i)`, mixing the predicted state `ẑ_i` with the posterior state `z_i`. Given the definition of the transition model in 2.3 (which uses `ẑ_t` as the prior/dynamics input), the rollout state at step `i` is `(ĥ_i, ẑ_i)`. A policy should be applied to the state available in the simulation (the predicted state), not a mismatched posterior state from the previous step.

comments.title: Underspecified Shield Introduction Schedule
comments.quote: To give the agent time to learn a good world model before restricting exploration through a learned shield, we augment the training procedure with Π which aims to gradually introduce shielding... start training with shielding disabled and enable shielding once the world model loss begins to plateau (see Appendix H for more details).
comments.explanation: While the authors mention introducing a 'novel' schedule, the condition 'once the world model loss begins to plateau' is not a precise, quantifiable condition. It relies on a subjective visual or monitoring criterion. For a rigorous method description, the trigger should be a specific parameter (e.g., epoch number, threshold on KL divergence) rather than a vague qualitative description. This makes the method difficult to reproduce or verify precisely.

comments.title: Loss Function Does Not Explicitly Show Class Weighting
comments.quote: Lviolation = α * BCE(lθ, Dλ) where the first three terms are as described in (Hafner et al., 2019a; 2021) and, for convenience, can be found in Appendix B. Lviolation is a new term that we introduce that acts as a weighted binary cross-entropy loss over predictions by lθ where Dλ ⊆S × {0, 1} is a dataset of state-label pairs (with 0 corresponding to safe and 1 to unsafe) and α ∈ [0, 1] is a weighting term. The inclusion of the weighting hyperparameter α is motivated by the observation that the number of observations of safe and unsafe states will generally be quite imbalanced.
comments.explanation: The text explicitly motivates a 'weighted binary cross-entropy' loss to handle class imbalance, but the equation `Lviolation = α * BCE(lθ, Dλ)` does not visually represent the weighting mechanism. `BCE` typically takes a binary label and a probability. The equation lacks the per-sample weights (e.g., `w_i` or `class_weights`) or a mechanism to define `Dλ` as weighted examples. While common shorthand exists, the equation is ambiguous regarding *how* the weights are applied compared to the text description.

comments.title: Variable Name Mismatch in Punishment Description
comments.quote: Whenever a violation occurs (whether detected by the latent shield or by the environment), we override the environment’s reward function by instead assigning a reward p < 0. This discourages the agent’s unshielded policy π from taking actions that lead to unsafe states through the standard RL setup.
comments.explanation: The prose refers to the punishment as 'p', stating the agent is assigned 'a reward p < 0'. However, in the input list for Algorithm 2, the parameter is named 'Punishment for violation rpunish'. The algorithm does not introduce a variable `p` in the provided snippet, creating a minor inconsistency between the prose description of the method and the formal algorithm input specification.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript addresses the critical challenge of safety-aware reinforcement learning in high-dimensional settings by proposing "latent shielding," a framework that integrates a learned recurrent state-space model with a safety filter to restrict actions without requiring a handcrafted dynamics model. The work is timely and the conceptual bridge between model-based representation learning and formal safety constraints is clearly motivated, with a logical structure and generally accessible exposition. However, the most significant issue is the inherent tension between the data-driven nature of the latent model and the formal rigor of the shielding approach; while the authors acknowledge the loss of formal verifiability, they do not sufficiently detail how the shield mitigates the risk of "hallucinated safety" arising from model inaccuracies or epistemic uncertainty, which poses a serious threat to robustness in real-world deployment. Furthermore, the reliance on a binary labelling function as a proxy for safety assumes access to a ground-truth oracle during training, which may be as impractical as learning the dynamics model in many scenarios, and this trade-off needs sharper critical analysis. Finally, the pun-based title is stylistically distracting for a top-tier venue, and the contribution regarding exploration would benefit from a clearer articulation of how it interacts with the safety constraints to prevent unsafe data collection during the learning phase.

comments.title: Inconsistent notation between environment states ($\mathcal{S}$) and latent states ($\mathcal{S}_I$)
comments.quote: states s ∈SE_, observations ot ∈OE_ where Dλ ⊂S × {0,1} is a dataset of state-label pairs
comments.explanation: The paper uses $\mathcal{S}$ to denote the environment state space in Section 2.1, but reuses $s_t$ (or $\mathcal{S}$) for compact latent states in $\mathcal{S}_I$ in Sections 3.1/3.2. This overload appears in key places: the dataset $\mathcal{D}_\lambda$ is defined as a subset of $\mathcal{S} \times \{0, 1\}$, despite the safety labeling function and SRSSM operating entirely in latent space. Readers will be confused about whether variables refer to physical environment states or internal latent representations. The authors should consistently use distinct symbols (e.g., $x_t$ or $s_t^I$ for latent states, and $\mathcal{S}_I$ for the latent space) to maintain notational rigor.

comments.title: Technical ambiguity in trajectory imagination step (posterior vs. prior state usage)
comments.quote: where ai = π ( ĥi, zi ) .
comments.explanation: In the imagination step, the trajectory is defined using predicted prior states $(\hat{h}_i, \hat{z}_i)$. However, the action selection is written as $a_i = \pi(\hat{h}_i, z_i)$, mixing the predicted deterministic state with the posterior stochastic state $z_i$ from the current timestep. The authors should use $\hat{z}_i$ to maintain notational and algorithmic consistency.

comments.explanation: Two formal safety definitions contain logical/notational flaws that undermine rigor. First, the $H$-bounded safe policy definition quantifies over $s \in S$ but uses $s_0$ in $\pi(s_0) = a_0$, likely a typo that breaks the logical flow. Second, the definition of an "inevitable violation" quantifies over $\varrho(s_0)$ (all finite trajectories), but the predicate $S_H$ and the shield's prescience are strictly defined over bounded trajectories of length $H$ ($\varrho^H(s)$). This creates a semantic mismatch where trajectories could exceed horizon $H$, leaving the predicate undefined. The authors should correct the index typo to $\pi(s)=a$ and align the inevitable violation quantifier with $\varrho^H(s_0)$.

Clarifying this contrast (e.g., 'achieves this while retaining an explicit, programmatic abstraction') would prevent readers from misinterpreting the relationship between prior methods and the necessity of an abstraction.

progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive Original (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive_original__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This manuscript addresses the critical challenge of safety-aware reinforcement learning in high-dimensional settings by proposing "latent shielding," a framework that integrates a learned recurrent state-space model with a safety filter to restrict actions without requiring a handcrafted dynamics model. The work is timely and the conceptual bridge between model-based representation learning and formal safety constraints is clearly motivated, with a logical structure and generally accessible exposition. However, the most significant issue is the inherent tension between the data-driven nature of the latent model and the formal rigor of the shielding approach; while the authors acknowledge the loss of formal verifiability, they do not sufficiently detail how the shield mitigates the risk of "hallucinated safety" arising from model inaccuracies or epistemic uncertainty, which poses a serious threat to robustness in real-world deployment. Furthermore, the reliance on a binary labelling function as a proxy for safety assumes access to a ground-truth oracle during training, which may be as impractical as learning the dynamics model in many scenarios, and this trade-off needs sharper critical analysis. Finally, the pun-based title is stylistically distracting for a top-tier venue, and the contribution regarding exploration would benefit from a clearer articulation of how it interacts with the safety constraints to prevent unsafe data collection during the learning phase.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency: Overloaded symbol for environment vs. latent states
comments.quote: states _s ∈SE_, observations _ot ∈OE_
comments.explanation: In Section 2.1, the authors explicitly define $s \in S_E$ as an environment state. However, in Sections 3.1 and 3.2, the symbol $s_t$ is reused to denote a compact latent state in $S_I$ (e.g., 'latent states _st ∈SI_'). This overloads a standard RL symbol and contradicts the earlier definition. Readers tracking state spaces will be confused about whether $s$ refers to the physical environment or the agent's internal model. The authors should use a distinct symbol (e.g., $x_t$, $\xi_t$, or $s_t^I$) for latent states to maintain notational rigor.

comments.title: Ambiguous phrasing regarding abstraction retention in prior work
comments.quote: To our knowledge, few works (Srinivasan et al., 2020; Thananjeyan et al., 2021; Bharadhwaj et al., 2021; Giacobbe et al., 2021) focus on removing the need for handcrafting an abstraction (among the most time-consuming and error-prone aspects of shielding), and only one of them (Giacobbe et al. (2021)) achieves this without getting rid of the abstraction altogether, albeit by providing the agent with access to the program that controls the environment.
comments.explanation: The clause 'achieves this without getting rid of the abstraction altogether' is syntactically confusing when 'this' refers back to 'removing the need for handcrafting an abstraction'. The intended contrast is that while most methods remove handcrafting by *also* abandoning abstractions (e.g., model-free or critic-based approaches), Giacobbe et al. remove handcrafting by *replacing* it with a simulation-derived abstraction. Clarifying this contrast (e.g., 'achieves this while retaining an explicit, programmatic abstraction') would prevent readers from misinterpreting the relationship between prior methods and the necessity of an abstraction.
